era: Seattle, Capitol Hill. Radio/Label. E=3 -- behind-the-scenes energy. KCMU DJ turned Sub Pop co-founder. The business architect of the Seattle sound. 1985-present.

location.formative: KCMU (University of Washington). The freeform radio station that broadcast underground Seattle music in the 1980s. Training ground for cultural curators. Where Poneman met Bruce Pavitt.
location.career: Sub Pop Records. Capitol Hill, Seattle. Co-founded 1986 with Bruce Pavitt. The label that defined the Seattle sound. Nirvana, Soundgarden, Mudhoney, Fleet Foxes, Beach House.

members.name: Jonathan Poneman
members.note: The business half of the Sub Pop partnership. KCMU DJ. Startup capital, promotional genius, financial strategist. Negotiated the Nirvana royalty deal. Engineered the Warner 49% partnership.

associated_musicians.name: Bruce Pavitt
associated_musicians.note: The curatorial half. Started Sub Pop as a fanzine at Evergreen State College. Identified the sound that became grunge. Consecutive degrees 127-128.

associated_musicians.name: Nasty Nes Rodriguez
associated_musicians.note: Hip-hop DJ at KCMU. Part of the KCMU/radio trio: Nasty Nes (126), Poneman (127), Pavitt (128). Three consecutive radio/label figures.

associated_musicians.name: Nirvana
associated_musicians.note: Bleach (1989). The Nirvana-to-DGC deal and Nevermind royalties saved Sub Pop from bankruptcy.

associated_musicians.name: Mudhoney
associated_musicians.note: Touch Me I'm Sick (1988). One of the earliest defining documents of the Seattle sound.

associated_musicians.name: Soundgarden
associated_musicians.note: Hunted Down (1988). Early Sub Pop single establishing the label's heavy, raw aesthetic.

associated_musicians.name: Fleet Foxes
associated_musicians.note: Baroque folk. The label's reinvention in the post-grunge era.

associated_musicians.name: Beach House
associated_musicians.note: Dream pop. Sub Pop's genre-agnostic expansion.

associated_musicians.name: Shabazz Palaces
associated_musicians.note: Experimental hip-hop. Sub Pop's most unexpected genre expansion.

theory_nodes.concept: Sub Pop founders enter the engine (127-128) -- landmark label founding pair: Poneman (business, 127) plus Pavitt (curatorial, 128) at consecutive degrees; the label that defined the Seattle sound; Nirvana, Soundgarden, Mudhoney, Fleet Foxes, Beach House; three decades; the founders arrive after the label has been referenced throughout the engine
examples: Jonathan Poneman (degree 127) -- business architect. KCMU DJ. Startup capital. Negotiated Nirvana royalty deal. Warner 49% partnership.
Bruce Pavitt (degree 128) -- curatorial visionary. Sub Pop fanzine. Evergreen State College. Identified the grunge sound.
Sub Pop roster across three decades: Mudhoney, Nirvana, Soundgarden, TAD, Postal Service, Shins, Fleet Foxes, Beach House, Shabazz Palaces, IDLES.
theory_nodes.description: Sub Pop founding pair enters at consecutive degrees. Business (127) + curatorial (128). The label that defined Seattle.

ancestors: Sub Pop label credits throughout the engine. The label has been present; now the founders are documented.
Kill Rock Stars entry at degree 124. Two legendary PNW independent labels in close proximity.
genealogy.relationship: Founding pair at degrees 127-128. Track Sub Pop founder references.

theory_nodes.concept: KCMU/radio trio (126-128) -- cultural pattern: three consecutive radio/label figures from the KCMU orbit; Nasty Nes Rodriguez (126, hip-hop DJ, E=4), Jonathan Poneman (127, label co-founder, E=3), Bruce Pavitt (128, label co-founder, E=3); energy descends from performer to infrastructure
examples: Nasty Nes (126, E=4) -- on-air DJ presence. Active performer. Hip-hop.
Poneman (127, E=3) -- behind-the-scenes. Business architect. Label infrastructure.
Pavitt (128, E=3) -- behind-the-scenes. Curatorial visionary. Fanzine/label.
KCMU as training ground for cultural curators across the Seattle music spectrum.
theory_nodes.description: KCMU/radio trio: three consecutive degrees, three radio/label figures. DJ to founders.

ancestors: KCMU/KEXP as cultural institution. The station that played the bands nobody else would play.
Radio figures in the engine: Nasty Nes (126) precedes the Sub Pop founders.
genealogy.relationship: Radio trio at degrees 126-128. Track KCMU/KEXP connections.

theory_nodes.concept: E=3 as infrastructure energy -- the quiet power of the behind-the-scenes architect; not low energy but foundational energy; the system that makes performance possible; without Poneman no Sub Pop, without Sub Pop no label for Nirvana
examples: Poneman at E=3: not a performer, not on stage. Business, marketing, negotiation.
Infrastructure energy: the checks that get signed, the distribution deals that get made, the promotional strategies that create visibility.
The Nirvana royalty negotiation: a single business decision that saved an entire label.
theory_nodes.description: E=3 as infrastructure energy. Foundational, not low. The system behind the sound.

ancestors: E=3 entries in the engine. Label and infrastructure figures at lower energy levels.
E=4 at Nasty Nes (126): the on-air DJ. E=3 at Poneman (127): the behind-the-scenes builder.
genealogy.relationship: Infrastructure energy at degree 127. Track at degree 128 (Pavitt also E=3).

cross_references.relationship: CO-FOUNDER PAIR! Poneman (business, 127) + Pavitt (curatorial, 128) = Sub Pop. Consecutive degrees for the founding partnership. The business architect meets the curatorial visionary.

KCMU TRIO! Three consecutive radio/label figures: Nasty Nes (126, E=4, hip-hop DJ), Poneman (127, E=3, label co-founder), Pavitt (128, E=3, label co-founder). Energy descends from performer to infrastructure.

LABEL CONTRAST. Kill Rock Stars (Olympia, 124) then Sub Pop founders (Seattle, 127-128). Two legendary PNW independent labels. KRS: independence as principle. Sub Pop: independence with pragmatic survival.

OLYMPIA LINEAGE. Calvin Johnson's K Records (43) provided the philosophical template for PNW independent labels. Sub Pop built on that template with business infrastructure K Records deliberately avoided.
